data.alert_description: Investigate operational state changes on Serial0/1/0 for R1 (oper up -> lower-layer-down, admin up) and R2 (oper up -> down, admin down); determine root cause and recommended remediation.

Observed interface Serial0/1/0 on router2: admin=down oper=down speed=2048000 phys_address=ac:3a:67:20:33:70
Observed interface Serial0/1/0 on router3: admin=up oper=up speed=2048000 phys_address=ac:3a:67:2c:e6:20

facts.claim: router1 Serial0/1/0 admin_state=up oper_state=lower-layer-down speed_bps=2064384 phys_address=ac:3a:67:20:22:90
router2 Serial0/1/0 admin_state=down oper_state=down speed_bps=2048000 phys_address=ac:3a:67:20:33:70
router3 Serial0/1/0 admin_state=up oper_state=up speed_bps=2048000 phys_address=ac:3a:67:2c:e6:20

root_causes.cause: Router2 Serial0/1/0 was administratively brought down, which drove its operational state to down and caused Router1's remote-facing Serial0/1/0 to transition to oper=lower-layer-down while the local admin state remained up.
evidence: router2 Serial0/1/0: admin_state=down oper_state=down last_change=2026-05-26T09:08:13.00044+00:00 (source: get_interface_status)
router1 Serial0/1/0: admin_state=up oper_state=lower-layer-down last_change=2026-05-26T09:08:17.00068+00:00 (source: get_interface_status)
Timing: R2 last_change (09:08:13) precedes R1 last_change (09:08:17) — consistent with remote admin down causing local carrier loss

root_causes.cause: No interface-level input/output/CRC errors or drops observed on the affected interfaces, and a peer (router3) shows Serial0/1/0 admin=up oper=up — consistent with an administrative action on R2 rather than a physical line or carrier fault.
evidence: router1 Serial0/1/0: input_errors=0 output_errors=0 drops=0 (source: get_interface_status)
router2 Serial0/1/0: input_errors=0 output_errors=0 drops=0 (source: get_interface_status)
router3 Serial0/1/0: admin_state=up oper_state=up (unchanged) — suggests the serial circuit or service is not globally down

data.intent_description: Router2 Serial0/1/0 is administratively down (admin=down, oper=down), which caused Router1 Serial0/1/0 to go oper=lower-layer-down while admin=up. Restore R2 interface admin up to re-establish carrier and return R1 oper to up.

plan.problem: Router2 Serial0/1/0 is administratively down, causing Router1 Serial0/1/0 to show lower-layer-down and loss of adjacency/carrier.
plan.fix_summary: Set the administrative state of Serial0/1/0 on router2 to up. Risk: brief traffic interruption on that interface during state change.

messages.content:
What was observed
- Router1 Serial0/1/0: admin=up, oper=lower-layer-down.
- Router2 Serial0/1/0: admin=down, oper=down (peer causing carrier loss).
- Router3 unaffected (admin/oper up).

Diagnosis (root cause)
- Remote administrative shutdown on Router2 Serial0/1/0 caused Router1 to lose carrier (oper lower-layer-down).

Changes made
- Brought Router2 Serial0/1/0 administratively up.

Verification
- Passed. Post-change checks: Router2 Serial0/1/0 -> admin=up oper=up; Router1 Serial0/1/0 -> admin=up oper=up.
- Remaining issues: none.
